proposal.reasoning: This is the initial exploration phase with no prior data. To effectively sample the parameter space and identify promising regions for acetone-like molecule optimization (target density 0.936 g/cm³), I will propose 4 diverse strategies: (1) Conservative baseline using mid-range values to establish a reference point, (2) High cohesion strategy with increased epsilon values (more negative) to boost density, HVap, and surface tension through stronger intermolecular attractions, (3) Tight packing strategy with reduced Rmin values to increase density via closer molecular packing, and (4) Aggressive combined strategy balancing strong attractions with moderately reduced sizes. This covers the primary axes of variation (epsilon vs. sigma) while keeping bond parameters within reasonable ranges to maintain molecular stability.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing epsilon parameters from weak to strong attraction (more negative values) will increase cohesion energy, directly impacting density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension. Simultaneously, varying Rmin parameters controls the excluded volume and packing efficiency; smaller Rmin values allow tighter packing and higher density. The combination of these parameters determines the balance between attractive forces and steric repulsion that governs liquid thermodynamics.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Fork 2 (high epsilon) should increase ΔH_vap and surface tension toward targets of 10.95 kcal/mol and 32.43 dyn/cm. Fork 3 (small Rmin) should boost density toward 0.936 g/cm³. By testing these orthogonal directions, we identify whether the system is attraction-limited or packing-limited, guiding subsequent refinement.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, measuring density, enthalpy of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment to determine which region of parameter space best reproduces target properties.

Simultaneously, increasing Rmin parameters (ACY_rminby2, DIM_rminby2, ACY_DIM_rmin) toward their maximum boundaries and increasing bond length (ACY_DIM_bl) will increase excluded volume, reducing the 20% density excess and potentially correcting the unphysical positive thermal expansion coefficient by reducing packing efficiency.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will decrease density toward the target (0.9361 g/cm³), reduce enthalpy of vaporization toward target (10.951 kcal/mol), lower surface tension toward target (32.43 dyn/cm), and restore physically correct liquid behavior where density decreases with increasing temperature.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, measuring density at 298K and 313K (to verify thermal expansion sign), enthalpy of vaporization, and surface tension. Fork 1 tests pure epsilon reduction, Fork 2 tests pure size increase, Fork 3 tests moderate combined adjustment, and Fork 4 tests extreme combined adjustment to identify which physical mechanism dominates the over-binding correction.

diagnostic.density_assessment: Significantly under-packed liquid (19% below target). Density follows correct temperature trend (decreases with T) but absolute value indicates atoms too large (Rmin too high) and/or attractions too weak (epsilon too small).
diagnostic.hvap_assessment: Below target by ~14.5% at both temperatures. Indicates insufficient intermolecular attraction strength. Epsilon values need to be more negative to increase cohesive energy.
diagnostic.surface_tension_assessment: Surprisingly accurate (2.4% high at 298K, 1.0% high at 313K) despite low density. This suggests competing errors or that the interface structure is fortuitously correct while bulk packing is wrong.

proposal.reasoning: The optimization has been stuck for 34 iterations with zero parameter variance, indicating a deep local minimum at the center of parameter space. The diagnostic reveals severe under-binding: density is 19% below target (excessive excluded volume from oversized Rmin values) and HVap is 14% too low (insufficient cohesive forces from weak epsilon values). To escape this plateau, I propose four maximally diverse strategies that push parameters toward their boundaries: (1) Pure cohesion strengthening by maximizing epsilon magnitudes to isolate the effect on HVap; (2) Pure size reduction by minimizing all Rmin values and bond length to isolate the effect on density; (3) Balanced compromise with moderate adjustments to both epsilon and size to find a synergistic middle ground; (4) Aggressive combined approach simultaneously applying minimum Rmin, maximum epsilon magnitude, and minimum bond length to test the physical limits of binding improvement.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Decreasing Rmin parameters (ACY_rminby2, DIM_rminby2, ACY_DIM_rmin) reduces excluded volume, allowing molecules to pack more tightly and increasing density toward the target 0.936 g/cm³. Increasing epsilon magnitudes (making ACY_epsilon, DIM_epsilon, ACY_DIM_epsilon more negative) strengthens London dispersion forces, increasing cohesive energy density and heat of vaporization toward 10.95 kcal/mol. Decreasing ACY_DIM_bl reduces molecular size and may improve packing efficiency. These effects are coupled: smaller Rmin values allow closer molecular approach, enhancing the effective attractive forces even at constant epsilon.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Fork 1 will test if HVap deficits can be resolved through cohesion alone; Fork 2 tests if density deficits can be resolved through packing alone; Fork 3 seeks a balanced trade-off to improve both properties moderately; Fork 4 tests the maximum achievable binding energy density within constraints. This design breaks the symmetry of the current plateau and identifies which physical mechanism (size vs. cohesion) dominates the under-binding error.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, measuring density at 298K and 313K, enthalpy of vaporization, and surface tension. Comparison will reveal whether density/HVap errors are more sensitive to size reduction (Fork 2), epsilon strengthening (Fork 1), or require combined adjustment (Forks 3-4).
